evidence_text: Across multiple coding agents and LLMs, context files tended to reduce task success rates versus providing no repository context, while increasing inference cost by over 20%.
source_excerpt: “Across multiple coding agents and LLMs, we find that context files tend to reduce task success rates compared to providing no repository context, while also increasing inference cost by over 20%.”
note: This is a top-level empirical result with a tradeoff; the abstract also presents a proposed explanation later rather than proving a complete mechanism here.
caveat: This is a top-level empirical result with a tradeoff; the abstract also presents a proposed explanation later rather than proving a complete mechanism here.

evidence_text: Developer-provided context files improved performance only marginally versus omitting context files, with an average increase of 4%.
source_excerpt: “developer-provided files only marginally improve performance compared to omitting them entirely (an increase of 4% on average)”
note: The source states this as an average across the evaluated settings.
caveat: The source states this as an average across the evaluated settings.

evidence_text: LLM-generated context files had a small negative effect on agent performance, with an average decrease of 3%.
source_excerpt: “while LLM-generated context files have a small negative effect on agent performance (a decrease of 3% on average)”
note: This result is in tension with developer recommendations to generate repository context files.
caveat: This result is in tension with developer recommendations to generate repository context files.

evidence_text: AGENTbench contains 138 unique Python software-engineering instances from 12 recent niche repositories that all include developer-written context files.
source_excerpt: “The benchmark contains 138 138 unique instances, covering both bug-fixing and feature addition tasks across 12 12 recent and niche repositories, which all feature developer-written context files.”
note: This describes the benchmark scope used for the developer-context-file evaluation setting.
caveat: This describes the benchmark scope used for the developer-context-file evaluation setting.

evidence_text: To keep a pull request for AGENTbench construction, the PR had to reference at least one issue, modify at least one Python file, and be assessed as introducing deterministic, testable behavior.
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, filtering pull requests
source_excerpt: “We only keep PRs that satisfy the following two criteria: they should reference at least one issue, and they should modify at least one Python file. Further, we filter for PRs that are assessed by the agent to introduce deterministic, testable behaviors that are suitable for SWE-bench Lite-like regression tests.”
note: The source contrasts this with stricter SWE-bench Lite practices on test edits.
caveat: The source contrasts this with stricter SWE-bench Lite practices on test edits.

evidence_text: The dataset kept only PRs whose setup script produced a machine-readable test-results dictionary containing at least one passing test, which held for 87% of filtered instances.
source_excerpt: “We only keep PRs where the resulting dictionary contains at least one passing test, which corresponds to 87 % 87\% of the filtered instances.”
note: This is a retention criterion in the benchmark construction pipeline.
caveat: This is a retention criterion in the benchmark construction pipeline.

evidence_text: The authors used an LLM agent to generate standardized task descriptions with six sections and reported that a random inspection of 10% of instances found no solution leakage.
source_excerpt: “This standardized task description is divided into 6 sections: description, steps to reproduce, expected behavior, observed behavior, specification, and additional information. Importantly, we ask the agent not to leak the solution in the generated task description, and to provide precise specifications. We randomly sampled and inspected 10% of the generated instances, and found that none of them leaked the solution.”
note: The no-leakage statement is based on inspection of a 10% sample, not the entire benchmark.
caveat: The no-leakage statement is based on inspection of a 10% sample, not the entire benchmark.

evidence_text: Generated tests were required to fail on the base repository state and pass on the patched state, and the resulting tests covered 75% of modified code on average.
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, generating unit tests
source_excerpt: “We verify that the added tests fail on R R and pass on R ∘ X ∗ R\circ X^{*} .” and “The resulting tests achieve an average coverage of 75% of the modified code (see Table ˜ 1 ).”
note: The source also states that manually improved tests were used to reduce over-specification.
caveat: The source also states that manually improved tests were used to reduce over-specification.

evidence_text: LLM-generated context files reduced average resolution rate by 0.5% on SWE-bench Lite and 2% on AGENTbench, while increasing average cost by 20% and 23% respectively.
source_excerpt: “In more detail, the average resolution rate is reduced by 0.5 % 0.5\% and 2 % 2\% on average on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench , respectively. Meanwhile, the context files increase the # steps in every setting on average by 2.45 2.45 and 3.92 3.92 steps, respectively, which leads to a cost increase of 20% and 23% on average, respectively”
note: This evidence states benchmark-level averages and shows a cost-performance tradeoff unfavorable to LLM-generated context files.
caveat: This evidence states benchmark-level averages and shows a cost-performance tradeoff unfavorable to LLM-generated context files.

evidence_text: Developer-provided context files outperformed LLM-generated ones for all four agents, improved performance over no context for all agents except Claude Code, and still increased average steps and cost.
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, human context files
source_excerpt: “We observe that the developer-provided context files outperform the LLM-generated ones for all four agents, despite not being agent-specific, and improve the performance compared to no context files for all agents but Claude Code (see Figure ˜ 3 right). However, developer-provided context files also increase the average number of steps and costs required to solve the task, on average by 3.34 3.34 steps and at most 19%, respectively.”
note: This evidence combines the comparative performance gain with the attached efficiency cost and one named exception.
caveat: This evidence combines the comparative performance gain with the attached efficiency cost and one named exception.

evidence_text: Context files did not meaningfully reduce the number of steps before agents first interacted with files modified in the original PR patch.
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, context files do not provide effective overviews
source_excerpt: “To assess the usefulness of these overviews, we measure how quickly agents discover files relevant to the described issue I I . Concretely, we measure the average number of steps before the coding agent interacts with any file modified in the original PR patch X ∗ X^{*} .” and “Both on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench the presence of context files does not meaningfully reduce this metric, as shown in Figure ˜ 4 .”
note: This evidence is bounded to one proxy for repository-overview usefulness: time to first relevant-file interaction.
caveat: This evidence is bounded to one proxy for repository-overview usefulness: time to first relevant-file interaction.

evidence_text: Manual trace inspection found that GPT-5.1 mini sometimes issued multiple commands to find context files and read them multiple times even though they were already included in context.
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, GPT-5.1 mini trace inspection
source_excerpt: “we observe in manual trace inspection that this increase is due to it (i) issuing multiple commands to find the context files and (ii) reading them (multiple times) despite them being already included in the agent’s context. Interestingly, we only observed this behavior if context files were present at all.”
note: This is a mechanism-relevant behavioral observation for one model-specific pattern rather than a universal behavior claim.
caveat: This is a mechanism-relevant behavioral observation for one model-specific pattern rather than a universal behavior claim.

evidence_text: When all other documentation was removed after context-file generation, LLM-generated context files improved performance by 2.7% on average and outperformed developer-written documentation.
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, redundant documentation experiment
source_excerpt: “In this setting, where context files are the only source of documentation available, LLM-generated context files not only consistently improve performance by 2.7 2.7 % on average, but also outperform developer-written documentation.”
note: This result depends on an altered documentation regime and is presented as a possible explanation for anecdotal reports of benefit.
caveat: This result depends on an altered documentation regime and is presented as a possible explanation for anecdotal reports of benefit.

evidence_text: With context files present, agents ran more tests, searched more files, read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling.
source_excerpt: “We find that, across all models, when context files are present, the coding agents run more tests. They also tend to navigate the repository more: they search more files ( grep ), read more files, and write more files. Lastly, adding context files causes agents to use more repository-specific tooling (e.g., uv and repo_tool ).”
note: This evidence describes trace-level behavioral changes associated with context-file presence.
caveat: This evidence describes trace-level behavioral changes associated with context-file presence.

evidence_text: Agents used tools much more often when those tools were named in context files, and GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini used more reasoning tokens when context files were present.
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, instruction following and reasoning tokens
source_excerpt: “Across multiple coding agents and LLMs, we find that context files tend to reduce task success rates compared to providing no repository context, while also increasing inference cost by over 20%.”
note: This is a top-level empirical result with a tradeoff; the abstract also presents a proposed explanation later rather than proving a complete mechanism here.
caveat: This is a top-level empirical result with a tradeoff; the abstract also presents a proposed explanation later rather than proving a complete mechanism here.
